content.body:
Excretion: removal of metabolic waste products

Main excretory organs:
Lungs: excrete CO₂ (from respiration)
Kidney: excrete urea, excess salts, water (urine)
Skin: excretes small amounts of urea, salts, water (sweat)
Liver: produces urea (deamination of excess amino acids)

Deamination: amino acid → organic acid + NH₃
NH₃ → urea (less toxic, soluble): 2NH₃+CO₂→CO(NH₂)₂+H₂O

content.title: Kidney Structure
content.body:
Renal cortex: contains glomeruli and Bowman's capsules
Renal medulla: loops of Henle and collecting ducts
Renal pelvis: collects urine → ureter

Nephron: functional unit of kidney (~1 million/kidney)
Glomerulus → Bowman's capsule → proximal convoluted tubule → loop of Henle → distal convoluted tubule → collecting duct

content.title: Kidney Functions
content.body:
1. Filtration (glomerulus): blood pressure filters small molecules
2. Selective reabsorption (PCT): glucose, amino acids, water, salts actively/passively reabsorbed
3. Secretion (DCT): active secretion of H⁺, K⁺, drugs
4. Concentration (Loop of Henle + collecting duct): creates osmotic gradient, ADH controls water reabsorption

questions.question: Excretion specifically means
options: removing metabolic waste products produced by chemical reactions in cells
questions.answer: removing metabolic waste products produced by chemical reactions in cells
questions.explanation: Excretion: CO₂ (respiration), urea (protein catabolism), bile pigments (haemoglobin breakdown). NOT faeces (that is egestion)

questions.question: Urea is produced in the liver by
questions.answer: deamination of excess amino acids (NH₃+CO₂→urea)
questions.explanation: Liver deaminates excess amino acids: removes NH₂ group → NH₃ → converted to less toxic urea via ornithine cycle

questions.question: Filtration in the kidney occurs at the
questions.answer: glomerulus / Bowman's capsule
questions.explanation: High blood pressure in glomerular capillaries forces small molecules (glucose, urea, salts, water) through into Bowman's capsule

questions.question: Which molecules are NOT filtered at glomerulus?
options: plasma proteins and blood cells (too large)
questions.answer: plasma proteins and blood cells (too large)
questions.explanation: Filtration is size-dependent: plasma proteins (albumin) and blood cells are too large to pass through filtration membrane

questions.question: Selective reabsorption in the proximal tubule returns glucose to blood by
questions.answer: active transport (requires energy/ATP)
questions.explanation: Glucose is actively transported from PCT lumen into blood — against concentration gradient — requires carrier proteins + ATP

questions.question: ADH (antidiuretic hormone) acts on
options: collecting duct and distal tubule — increases water reabsorption
questions.answer: collecting duct and distal tubule — increases water reabsorption
questions.explanation: ADH from posterior pituitary: makes collecting duct more permeable to water → more water reabsorbed → concentrated urine

questions.question: On a hot day with heavy sweating, what happens to ADH secretion?
questions.answer: ADH increases (dehydration detected → conserve water)
questions.explanation: Dehydration → blood becomes more concentrated (high osmolarity) → osmoreceptors in hypothalamus → more ADH released

questions.question: Loop of Henle's main function is
options: creating osmotic gradient in medulla enabling production of concentrated urine
questions.answer: creating osmotic gradient in medulla enabling production of concentrated urine
questions.explanation: Loop: descending limb (water leaves), ascending limb (NaCl pumped out) → creates high osmolarity in medulla → drives water reabsorption

questions.question: Kidney failure is treated by
options: dialysis (filters blood artificially) or kidney transplant
questions.answer: dialysis (filters blood artificially) or kidney transplant
questions.explanation: Dialysis: blood passed over semipermeable membrane, waste diffuses into dialysate. Kidney transplant is preferred long-term solution

questions.question: Which substance in urine indicates diabetes mellitus (Type 1)?
options: Glucose (not normally present — only if blood glucose exceeds renal threshold)
questions.answer: Glucose (not normally present — only if blood glucose exceeds renal threshold)
questions.explanation: Normally all filtered glucose is reabsorbed. In uncontrolled diabetes: blood glucose very high → exceeds transport capacity → glucose in urine (glycosuria)

questions.question: What is the basic unit of life?
questions.answer: The cell
questions.explanation: The cell is the basic structural and functional unit of all living organisms.

questions.question: Which organelle is responsible for energy production in cells?
questions.answer: Mitochondria
questions.explanation: Mitochondria carry out aerobic respiration to produce ATP — the cell's energy currency. Hence called the 'powerhouse of the cell'.

questions.question: What is photosynthesis?
options: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
The breakdown of glucose for energy
The absorption of minerals from soil
questions.answer: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
questions.explanation: Photosynthesis: 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + light energy → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂. Plants convert light energy to chemical energy stored in glucose.

questions.question: What is osmosis?
options: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.answer: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.explanation: Osmosis is the passive movement of water molecules through a semi-permeable membrane from a region of higher water potential to lower.

questions.question: What molecule carries genetic information?
questions.answer: DNA (Deoxyribonucleic acid)
questions.explanation: DNA carries the genetic code in sequences of bases (A, T, G, C) that determine the structure of proteins.

questions.question: What is the function of chloroplasts?
questions.answer: Site of photosynthesis
questions.explanation: Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll and are the site of photosynthesis, capturing light energy to make glucose.

questions.question: In genetics, what is a dominant allele?
options: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
One that is only expressed when two copies are present
questions.answer: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
questions.explanation: A dominant allele is expressed in the phenotype regardless of whether it is heterozygous or homozygous.

questions.question: What is diffusion?
options: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.answer: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.explanation: Diffusion is the passive movement of particles down a concentration gradient — no energy (ATP) is required.

questions.question: Which blood component carries oxygen?
questions.answer: Red blood cells (haemoglobin)
questions.explanation: Red blood cells contain haemoglobin, which binds to oxygen in the lungs and releases it in body tissues.

questions.question: What is the role of the nucleus?
options: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.answer: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.explanation: The nucleus is the control centre of the cell, containing DNA and directing all cellular activities including protein synthesis.

content.body:
Osmoregulation: maintaining constant water/solute balance in body fluids
Normal blood osmolarity: ~300 mOsm/L

Osmoreceptors in hypothalamus detect blood osmolarity
→ ADH from posterior pituitary

High solute/dehydration:
ADH ↑ → more water reabsorbed → concentrated urine (small volume)

Low solute/overhydration:
ADH ↓ → less water reabsorbed → dilute urine (large volume)

content.title: Comparing Kidney Adaptations
content.body:
Desert animals (e.g. kangaroo rat): very long loop of Henle → extremely concentrated urine → minimal water loss
Aquatic animals (e.g. freshwater fish): lots of dilute urine, actively absorb salts
Marine fish: drink seawater, excrete concentrated urine, secrete excess salt via gills

content.title: Control of Blood Glucose
content.body:
Pancreas: islets of Langerhans
Alpha cells: glucagon (raises blood glucose)
Beta cells: insulin (lowers blood glucose)

After meal: glucose ↑ → beta cells secrete insulin → cells take up glucose → glucose ↓
Fasting: glucose ↓ → alpha cells secrete glucagon → liver glycogen broken down → glucose ↑

Type 1 diabetes: no insulin (autoimmune β cell destruction)
Type 2: insulin resistance

questions.question: Osmoregulation controls
options: water and solute balance in body fluids
questions.answer: water and solute balance in body fluids
questions.explanation: Osmoregulation: kidneys regulate water/solute concentrations to maintain homeostatic blood osmolarity (~300 mOsm/L)

questions.question: Osmoreceptors detecting dehydration are located in
questions.answer: hypothalamus
questions.explanation: Osmoreceptors in hypothalamus shrink when blood is too concentrated → trigger ADH release from posterior pituitary

questions.question: Desert mammals have long loops of Henle to
options: create steeper osmotic gradient in medulla → produce very concentrated urine, conserving water
questions.answer: create steeper osmotic gradient in medulla → produce very concentrated urine, conserving water
questions.explanation: Longer loop of Henle: larger countercurrent multiplier effect → higher medullary osmolarity → more water reabsorbed → more concentrated urine

questions.question: Diabetes insipidus (not mellitus) is caused by
options: lack of ADH or ADH receptor defect → very large volumes of dilute urine
questions.answer: lack of ADH or ADH receptor defect → very large volumes of dilute urine
questions.explanation: Without ADH: collecting duct impermeable to water → massive dilute urine output (polyuria) → severe dehydration

questions.question: Insulin causes blood glucose to fall by
options: stimulating uptake of glucose by liver and muscle cells (→glycogen)
questions.answer: stimulating uptake of glucose by liver and muscle cells (→glycogen)
questions.explanation: Insulin binds receptors → GLUT4 transporters move to cell surface → glucose enters cells → blood glucose ↓

questions.question: Glucagon is released when blood glucose is
questions.answer: low (between meals/fasting)
questions.explanation: Alpha cells release glucagon when blood glucose falls → liver breaks down glycogen → glucose release → blood glucose ↑

questions.question: Type 1 diabetes differs from Type 2 in that
options: Type 1 involves autoimmune destruction of beta cells → no insulin produced
questions.answer: Type 1 involves autoimmune destruction of beta cells → no insulin produced
questions.explanation: Type 1: immune system destroys β cells → lifelong insulin injections required. Type 2: cells become resistant to insulin

questions.question: Negative feedback in blood glucose regulation means
options: when glucose rises, insulin brings it back down; when glucose falls, glucagon raises it
questions.answer: when glucose rises, insulin brings it back down; when glucose falls, glucagon raises it
questions.explanation: Negative feedback: deviation from set point → corrective response. Insulin corrects high glucose; glucagon corrects low glucose

questions.question: Marine fish avoid dehydration despite salty seawater by
options: drinking seawater and excreting excess salt via gills and kidneys
questions.answer: drinking seawater and excreting excess salt via gills and kidneys
questions.explanation: Marine bony fish: continual osmotic water loss to salty environment → must drink seawater, then work to excrete excess salt

questions.question: Urine composition test: presence of protein suggests
options: glomerulonephritis (damaged glomerulus allowing plasma proteins through)
questions.answer: glomerulonephritis (damaged glomerulus allowing plasma proteins through)
questions.explanation: Normal: no protein in urine. Proteinuria: damaged glomerular filtration barrier (inflammation/disease) allows albumin through

questions.answer: The cell
questions.explanation: The cell is the basic structural and functional unit of all living organisms.

questions.explanation: DNA carries the genetic code in sequences of bases (A, T, G, C) that determine the structure of proteins.

questions.answer: Site of photosynthesis
questions.explanation: Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll and are the site of photosynthesis, capturing light energy to make glucose.

options: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
One that is only expressed when two copies are present
questions.answer: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
questions.explanation: A dominant allele is expressed in the phenotype regardless of whether it is heterozygous or homozygous.

options: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.answer: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.explanation: Diffusion is the passive movement of particles down a concentration gradient — no energy (ATP) is required.

questions.question: Which blood component carries oxygen?
questions.answer: Red blood cells (haemoglobin)
questions.explanation: Red blood cells contain haemoglobin, which binds to oxygen in the lungs and releases it in body tissues.

questions.question: What is the role of the nucleus?
options: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.answer: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.explanation: The nucleus is the control centre of the cell, containing DNA and directing all cellular activities including protein synthesis.

content.body: In this lesson, we explore advanced aspects of Excretion. Build on what you have already learned and apply your knowledge to more challenging problems.
Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson you should be able to: (1) Apply Excretion concepts to complex problems. (2) Use appropriate formulas and methods. (3) Interpret results in context. (4) Connect this topic to related areas of biology.
Core Theory

This section of Excretion builds on foundational knowledge. In biology, advanced problem-solving requires not just knowing formulas but understanding when and how to apply them. Study each worked example carefully.

content.title: Worked Example 3.1
content.body: Consider a more complex problem on Excretion. Approach: (1) Read and understand the problem. (2) Identify all given information. (3) Determine which concepts apply. (4) Solve systematically, showing all steps. (5) Check your answer is reasonable.

content.title: Worked Example 3.2
content.body: Another type of problem you may encounter: Questions may test your ability to combine multiple concepts within Excretion. Practice identifying the type of problem and the appropriate method before attempting to solve.
Common examination questions on Excretion include: definitions and explanations, numerical calculations, diagram interpretation, and analysis of given data. Make sure you can handle all types.
Summary and Review
You have now covered all lessons in Excretion. Review the key concepts from each lesson, practice past paper questions, and ensure you can apply your knowledge confidently. Good luck in your studies!

questions.question: What is the basic unit of life?
questions.answer: The cell
questions.explanation: The cell is the basic structural and functional unit of all living organisms.

questions.question: Which organelle is responsible for energy production in cells?
questions.answer: Mitochondria
questions.explanation: Mitochondria carry out aerobic respiration to produce ATP — the cell's energy currency. Hence called the 'powerhouse of the cell'.

questions.question: What is photosynthesis?
options: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
The breakdown of glucose for energy
The absorption of minerals from soil
questions.answer: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
questions.explanation: Photosynthesis: 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + light energy → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂. Plants convert light energy to chemical energy stored in glucose.

questions.question: What is osmosis?
options: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.answer: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.explanation: Osmosis is the passive movement of water molecules through a semi-permeable membrane from a region of higher water potential to lower.

questions.question: What molecule carries genetic information?
questions.answer: DNA (Deoxyribonucleic acid)
questions.explanation: DNA carries the genetic code in sequences of bases (A, T, G, C) that determine the structure of proteins.

questions.question: What is the function of chloroplasts?
questions.answer: Site of photosynthesis
questions.explanation: Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll and are the site of photosynthesis, capturing light energy to make glucose.

questions.question: In genetics, what is a dominant allele?
options: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
One that is only expressed when two copies are present
questions.answer: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
questions.explanation: A dominant allele is expressed in the phenotype regardless of whether it is heterozygous or homozygous.

questions.question: What is diffusion?
options: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.answer: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.explanation: Diffusion is the passive movement of particles down a concentration gradient — no energy (ATP) is required.

questions.question: Which blood component carries oxygen?
questions.answer: Red blood cells (haemoglobin)
questions.explanation: Red blood cells contain haemoglobin, which binds to oxygen in the lungs and releases it in body tissues.

questions.question: What is the role of the nucleus?
options: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.answer: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.explanation: The nucleus is the control centre of the cell, containing DNA and directing all cellular activities including protein synthesis.

questions.question: What is respiration?
options: The process of releasing energy from glucose in cells
questions.answer: The process of releasing energy from glucose in cells
questions.explanation: Cellular respiration: C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂ → 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + ATP. It releases energy from glucose for cell use.

questions.question: What is natural selection?
options: Organisms with favourable traits survive and reproduce more
All organisms evolving at the same rate
questions.answer: Organisms with favourable traits survive and reproduce more
questions.explanation: Natural selection is Darwin's mechanism: individuals with traits better suited to the environment survive and pass on those traits.

questions.question: What organ filters blood in humans?
questions.answer: Kidneys
questions.explanation: The kidneys filter blood to remove waste products (urea, excess water, salts), producing urine.

questions.question: What is the function of stomata in leaves?
questions.answer: Gas exchange and transpiration
questions.explanation: Stomata are pores in leaves that allow CO₂ in and O₂ out during photosynthesis, and water vapour out during transpiration.

questions.question: Which part of the neurone carries impulses away from the cell body?
questions.answer: Axon
questions.explanation: The axon is the long fibre that carries nerve impulses away from the cell body to other neurons or effectors.

questions.question: What is the function of enzymes?
options: Biological catalysts that speed up reactions
questions.answer: Biological catalysts that speed up reactions
questions.explanation: Enzymes are protein catalysts that lower activation energy and speed up biochemical reactions without being consumed.

questions.question: What gas do plants absorb during photosynthesis?
questions.answer: Carbon dioxide (CO₂)
questions.explanation: Plants absorb CO₂ from the air through stomata during photosynthesis, converting it to glucose using light energy.

questions.question: What is a food chain?
options: A sequence showing how energy passes from one organism to another
A list of foods animals eat
questions.answer: A sequence showing how energy passes from one organism to another
questions.explanation: A food chain shows the transfer of energy from producers (plants) through consumers. Example: Grass → Rabbit → Fox.

questions.question: What is the function of the cell membrane?
options: Controls what enters and leaves the cell (selectively permeable)
questions.answer: Controls what enters and leaves the cell (selectively permeable)
questions.explanation: The cell membrane is selectively permeable, controlling the passage of substances in and out of the cell.

questions.question: What is mitosis?
options: Cell division producing two genetically identical daughter cells
questions.answer: Cell division producing two genetically identical daughter cells
questions.explanation: Mitosis is cell division for growth and repair, producing two daughter cells with identical chromosomes to the parent cell (diploid).

All organisms evolving at the same rate
questions.answer: Organisms with favourable traits survive and reproduce more
questions.explanation: Natural selection is Darwin's mechanism: individuals with traits better suited to the environment survive and pass on those traits.

questions.question: What organ filters blood in humans?
questions.answer: Kidneys
questions.explanation: The kidneys filter blood to remove waste products (urea, excess water, salts), producing urine.

questions.question: What is the function of stomata in leaves?
questions.answer: Gas exchange and transpiration
questions.explanation: Stomata are pores in leaves that allow CO₂ in and O₂ out during photosynthesis, and water vapour out during transpiration.

questions.question: Which part of the neurone carries impulses away from the cell body?
questions.answer: Axon
questions.explanation: The axon is the long fibre that carries nerve impulses away from the cell body to other neurons or effectors.

questions.question: What is the function of enzymes?
options: Biological catalysts that speed up reactions
questions.answer: Biological catalysts that speed up reactions
questions.explanation: Enzymes are protein catalysts that lower activation energy and speed up biochemical reactions without being consumed.

questions.question: What gas do plants absorb during photosynthesis?
questions.answer: Carbon dioxide (CO₂)
questions.explanation: Plants absorb CO₂ from the air through stomata during photosynthesis, converting it to glucose using light energy.

questions.question: What is a food chain?
options: A sequence showing how energy passes from one organism to another
A list of foods animals eat
questions.answer: A sequence showing how energy passes from one organism to another
questions.explanation: A food chain shows the transfer of energy from producers (plants) through consumers. Example: Grass → Rabbit → Fox.

questions.question: What is the function of the cell membrane?
options: Controls what enters and leaves the cell (selectively permeable)
questions.answer: Controls what enters and leaves the cell (selectively permeable)
questions.explanation: The cell membrane is selectively permeable, controlling the passage of substances in and out of the cell.

questions.question: What is mitosis?
options: Cell division producing two genetically identical daughter cells
questions.answer: Cell division producing two genetically identical daughter cells
questions.explanation: Mitosis is cell division for growth and repair, producing two daughter cells with identical chromosomes to the parent cell (diploid).

content.body: In this lesson, we explore advanced aspects of Excretion. Build on what you have already learned and apply your knowledge to more challenging problems.
Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson you should be able to: (1) Apply Excretion concepts to complex problems. (2) Use appropriate formulas and methods. (3) Interpret results in context. (4) Connect this topic to related areas of biology.
Core Theory

This section of Excretion builds on foundational knowledge. In biology, advanced problem-solving requires not just knowing formulas but understanding when and how to apply them. Study each worked example carefully.

content.title: Worked Example 6.1
content.body: Consider a more complex problem on Excretion. Approach: (1) Read and understand the problem. (2) Identify all given information. (3) Determine which concepts apply. (4) Solve systematically, showing all steps. (5) Check your answer is reasonable.

content.title: Worked Example 6.2
content.body: Another type of problem you may encounter: Questions may test your ability to combine multiple concepts within Excretion. Practice identifying the type of problem and the appropriate method before attempting to solve.
Common examination questions on Excretion include: definitions and explanations, numerical calculations, diagram interpretation, and analysis of given data. Make sure you can handle all types.
Summary and Review
You have now covered all lessons in Excretion. Review the key concepts from each lesson, practice past paper questions, and ensure you can apply your knowledge confidently. Good luck in your studies!

questions.question: What is the basic unit of life?
questions.answer: The cell
questions.explanation: The cell is the basic structural and functional unit of all living organisms.

questions.question: Which organelle is responsible for energy production in cells?
questions.answer: Mitochondria
questions.explanation: Mitochondria carry out aerobic respiration to produce ATP — the cell's energy currency. Hence called the 'powerhouse of the cell'.

questions.question: What is photosynthesis?
options: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
The breakdown of glucose for energy
The absorption of minerals from soil
questions.answer: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
questions.explanation: Photosynthesis: 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + light energy → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂. Plants convert light energy to chemical energy stored in glucose.

questions.question: What is osmosis?
options: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.answer: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.explanation: Osmosis is the passive movement of water molecules through a semi-permeable membrane from a region of higher water potential to lower.

questions.question: What molecule carries genetic information?
questions.answer: DNA (Deoxyribonucleic acid)
questions.explanation: DNA carries the genetic code in sequences of bases (A, T, G, C) that determine the structure of proteins.

questions.question: What is the function of chloroplasts?
questions.answer: Site of photosynthesis
questions.explanation: Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll and are the site of photosynthesis, capturing light energy to make glucose.

questions.question: In genetics, what is a dominant allele?
options: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
One that is only expressed when two copies are present
questions.answer: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
questions.explanation: A dominant allele is expressed in the phenotype regardless of whether it is heterozygous or homozygous.

questions.question: What is diffusion?
options: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.answer: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.explanation: Diffusion is the passive movement of particles down a concentration gradient — no energy (ATP) is required.

questions.question: Which blood component carries oxygen?
questions.answer: Red blood cells (haemoglobin)
questions.explanation: Red blood cells contain haemoglobin, which binds to oxygen in the lungs and releases it in body tissues.

questions.question: What is the role of the nucleus?
options: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.answer: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.explanation: The nucleus is the control centre of the cell, containing DNA and directing all cellular activities including protein synthesis.

questions.question: What is respiration?
options: The process of releasing energy from glucose in cells
questions.answer: The process of releasing energy from glucose in cells
questions.explanation: Cellular respiration: C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂ → 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + ATP. It releases energy from glucose for cell use.

questions.question: What is natural selection?
options: Organisms with favourable traits survive and reproduce more
All organisms evolving at the same rate
questions.answer: Organisms with favourable traits survive and reproduce more
questions.explanation: Natural selection is Darwin's mechanism: individuals with traits better suited to the environment survive and pass on those traits.

questions.question: What organ filters blood in humans?
questions.answer: Kidneys
questions.explanation: The kidneys filter blood to remove waste products (urea, excess water, salts), producing urine.

questions.question: What is the function of stomata in leaves?
questions.answer: Gas exchange and transpiration
questions.explanation: Stomata are pores in leaves that allow CO₂ in and O₂ out during photosynthesis, and water vapour out during transpiration.

questions.question: Which part of the neurone carries impulses away from the cell body?
questions.answer: Axon
questions.explanation: The axon is the long fibre that carries nerve impulses away from the cell body to other neurons or effectors.

questions.question: What is the function of enzymes?
options: Biological catalysts that speed up reactions
questions.answer: Biological catalysts that speed up reactions
questions.explanation: Enzymes are protein catalysts that lower activation energy and speed up biochemical reactions without being consumed.

questions.question: What gas do plants absorb during photosynthesis?
questions.answer: Carbon dioxide (CO₂)
questions.explanation: Plants absorb CO₂ from the air through stomata during photosynthesis, converting it to glucose using light energy.

questions.question: What is a food chain?
options: A sequence showing how energy passes from one organism to another
A list of foods animals eat
questions.answer: A sequence showing how energy passes from one organism to another
questions.explanation: A food chain shows the transfer of energy from producers (plants) through consumers. Example: Grass → Rabbit → Fox.

questions.question: What is the function of the cell membrane?
options: Controls what enters and leaves the cell (selectively permeable)
questions.answer: Controls what enters and leaves the cell (selectively permeable)
questions.explanation: The cell membrane is selectively permeable, controlling the passage of substances in and out of the cell.

questions.question: What is mitosis?
options: Cell division producing two genetically identical daughter cells
questions.answer: Cell division producing two genetically identical daughter cells
questions.explanation: Mitosis is cell division for growth and repair, producing two daughter cells with identical chromosomes to the parent cell (diploid).
